availabilityNux.message: Our most capable model yet. GPT-5.6 Sol can tackle complex code changes, dig into research, produce polished documents, and take on your most ambitious work. Sol is highly capable at lower reasoning efforts—try starting lower, then turn it up for harder jobs.

content.text: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is
Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

item.text:
Canada is the world’s second-largest country by total area, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean and northward to the Arctic Ocean. It is a federal parliamentary democracy, a constitutional monarchy, and one of the world’s most geographically, culturally, and economically diverse nations.

Canada’s capital is Ottawa, located in Ontario along the Ottawa River. Its largest city is Toronto, while other major urban centres include Montréal, Vancouver, Calgary, Edmonton, Québec City, Winnipeg, and Halifax. The country consists of ten provinces and three territories:

- Provinces: British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Québec, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador.
- Territories: Yukon, the Northwest Territories, and Nunavut.

Canada has a population of roughly 40 million people, concentrated mainly near the southern border with the United States. Its territory is enormous, but much of the north is sparsely populated because of its Arctic climate, rugged terrain, and limited transportation infrastructure.

Geography and nature

Canada contains an extraordinary range of landscapes:

- The Atlantic provinces feature rocky coastlines, fishing communities, forests, and dramatic ocean scenery.
- Québec and Ontario contain the Great Lakes–St. Lawrence region, one of the country’s most populated and industrialized areas.
- The Prairie provinces—Manitoba, Saskatchewan, and Alberta—are known for broad grasslands, fertile farmland, and major agricultural production.
- British Columbia includes Pacific coastline, temperate rainforests, islands, and the Coast Mountains.
- The Rocky Mountains dominate parts of Alberta and British Columbia, with famous national parks such as Banff and Jasper.
- The northern territories contain tundra, boreal forest, glaciers, immense rivers, and the Northwest Passage.
- Hudson Bay, one of the world’s largest inland seas, reaches deep into central Canada.

Canada has more lakes than any other country. The Great Lakes—Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario—form one of the largest freshwater systems on Earth, although Lake Michigan lies entirely within the United States. Canada also contains vast forests, especially the boreal forest, which stretches across much of the country and stores enormous amounts of carbon.

Wildlife includes moose, caribou, grizzly bears, polar bears, black bears, wolves, beavers, lynx, bison, whales, seals, bald eagles, and many migratory birds. The beaver is a national symbol and appears on the Canadian five-cent coin.

History

Indigenous peoples have lived in the lands now called Canada for thousands of years. They include First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples, each with distinct cultures, languages, political traditions, and histories. Indigenous societies developed complex systems of governance, trade, agriculture, hunting, fishing, diplomacy, and environmental stewardship.

European exploration began prominently with Norse voyages around the year 1000 and later with expeditions by John Cabot, Jacques Cartier, Samuel de Champlain, and others. France established colonies along the St. Lawrence River and in Acadia, while Britain expanded its presence through trade, settlement, and military conflict.

The British conquest of New France in 1760 profoundly shaped the country. Québec retained much of its French language, civil law, Catholic heritage, and cultural identity. This French-English duality remains one of Canada’s defining characteristics.

Canada was created as a federation in 1867 through Confederation. Ontario, Québec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick formed the original Dominion of Canada. Other provinces and territories joined gradually, with Newfoundland entering in 1949 and Nunavut being created in 1999.

Canada’s history also includes severe injustices against Indigenous peoples. The residential-school system forcibly separated many Indigenous children from their families and attempted to suppress their languages and cultures. Its effects continue today, and reconciliation remains a major national issue.

Government and political life

Canada has a federal system in which powers are divided between the national government, provinces, and territories. The federal Parliament has two chambers:

- The House of Commons, whose members are elected.
- The Senate, whose members are appointed.

The prime minister leads the federal government and is normally the leader of the political party able to command the confidence of the House of Commons. The monarch is Canada’s head of state, represented federally by the governor general.

Provincial governments control important areas such as education, health care administration, natural resources, and many aspects of civil law. Municipal governments manage services including roads, public transit, water systems, zoning, and local policing.

Canada is officially bilingual at the federal level, with English and French recognized as official languages. Québec is predominantly French-speaking, while English is dominant in most other provinces. Many Canadians also speak languages such as Mandarin, Punjabi, Spanish, Arabic, Tagalog, Italian, German, and Ukrainian. Indigenous languages include Inuktitut, Cree, Ojibwe, Dene languages, and many others.

Economy

Canada has a wealthy, highly developed mixed economy. Major sectors include:

- Energy, including oil, natural gas, hydroelectricity, and renewable power.
- Mining, with production of gold, nickel, copper, uranium, potash, iron ore, and other minerals.
- Agriculture, especially wheat, canola, livestock, dairy, and food processing.
- Forestry and pulp and paper.
- Manufacturing, including automobiles, aerospace, machinery, chemicals, and food products.
- Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, software, telecommunications, gaming, and clean technology.
- Finance and professional services.
- Tourism, education, health care, and public administration.

Canada trades extensively with the United States, which is its largest economic partner. The two countries share one of the world’s longest international borders and are deeply connected through supply chains, energy infrastructure, transportation networks, culture, and family ties.

Society and culture

Canadian culture is shaped by Indigenous traditions, French and British histories, immigration from around the world, and strong regional identities. Canada is one of the world’s most multicultural countries, and immigration has played a central role in its population growth and social development.

Canadian cultural contributions include literature by Margaret Atwood, Alice Munro, Michael Ondaatje, and Yann Martel; music by Leonard Cohen, Joni Mitchell, Céline Dion, Drake, The Weeknd, and Shania Twain; and influential film, television, comedy, visual art, and architecture.

Hockey is widely regarded as Canada’s signature sport, but basketball, soccer, baseball, curling, lacrosse, skiing, snowboarding, and Canadian football are also important. Lacrosse is Canada’s national summer sport, while hockey is its national winter sport.

Canadian food traditions vary by region. Examples include Québec’s poutine, Montréal-style bagels and smoked meat, Atlantic seafood, Prairie grains and beef, British Columbia salmon, butter tarts, Nanaimo bars, maple syrup, and bannock. Maple syrup, produced primarily in Québec and Ontario, is an internationally recognized Canadian product.

Climate

Canada experiences nearly every climate zone. Coastal British Columbia has relatively mild, rainy winters, while the Prairies can experience very cold winters and hot summers. Québec and Ontario have humid continental climates with pronounced seasonal changes. Atlantic Canada has variable maritime weather, and the territories experience long, severe winters.

Climate change is especially significant for Canada because the Arctic is warming rapidly. Consequences include melting permafrost, shrinking glaciers, changing wildlife habitats, coastal erosion, wildfire risk, flooding, and disruptions to northern communities. At the same time, Canada possesses substantial freshwater, forest, mineral, and renewable-energy resources that are important to global climate and energy discussions.

National identity

Canada is often associated with vast wilderness, public health care, multiculturalism, bilingualism, peacekeeping, winter sports, and close relations with the United States. Yet Canada is not culturally uniform. Its identity differs substantially between Québec, the Atlantic provinces, the Prairies, British Columbia, Ontario, and the northern territories.

At its best, Canada presents itself as a country built around pluralism: different peoples, languages, regions, and traditions sharing political institutions. Its central challenges include reconciliation with Indigenous peoples, regional tensions, housing affordability, immigration and infrastructure pressures, climate change, health-care capacity, and balancing economic development with environmental protection.
